name: Abilities
definition:
An ability can be line(s) of text printed on a card, or given to a card via certain keywords or effects.

Abilities are either played or continuous. (see: Played Abilities and Continuous Abilities)

name: Ability That _____
definition:
If an effect refers to "an ability that" does something, it is referring to the possible results of the ability in general. If an ability can cause a result in any situation, it counts as an ability that causes that result. It does not matter what the result of one specific use of the ability is.

This ability has the possible result of reducing damage. Therefore it counts as "an ability that reduces damage". It also counts as an ability that increases speed, among other things.

Therefore, the above enhance on You're So Obvious [MHA01-9] can be responded to by Fighting Spirit [MHA02-53] which has the ability 'Response Flip: After your rival plays a non-character ability that reduces the damage of an attack, cancel it.')
examples: You're So Obvious has the ability 'Enhance Destroy: Return this attack to its printed damage. If it is a Punch attack, it gets +2 speed.'.

name: Action
definition:
Actions are one of the six card types.

Playing and passing an action card is an added cost to playing one of its abilities from your hand. You can't play the card otherwise, save for blocking with it.

You play an action card's abilities from your hand. You can't normally play them from anywhere else.

play an ability on an Action card in that zone, provided it follows all other rules on playing an action. (For instance, you cannot play a Form during the Enhance Step, or an Enhance during a Response window.)

If an ability on an action has costs, you pay those costs before trying to play the action. If you fail the check to play it, any other costs you've paid are still spent.
examples: Capture Evil-Doers has a Form ability and an Enhance ability. This means you can play one of those abilities straight from your hand, at the usual timing for each type of ability. When you do, you will need to play Capture Evil-Doers into your card pool, and pass a check against a difficulty of 2 plus progressive. If you fail, discard the action card, and you don't get the effect of the ability you were trying to play.
bullets: If an effect allows you to play cards from another specific zone, you may

name: Active Player
definition:
The player whose turn it is.

The active player:
bullets: Has the first priority to play a response ability in any response window.
Clears their card pool first.
Handles their fired, modifier, and rule effects first (in any order they choose), when both players have such effects to apply.

name: Additional Costs
definition:
Additional costs are costs created by card effects. They must be paid when taking the described action.

Additional costs to play a card are paid before you make the control check.

name: Ally
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: As Though
definition: Some effects state that a player may do something "as though" some condition were true or a player can do something "as though" some condition were true. This applies only to the stated effect. For purposes of that effect, treat the game exactly as if the stated condition were true. For all other purposes, treat the game normally.
examples: Spellstorm allows a player to flip Spell cards in their card pool "as though you were spending momentum to pay for this attack's keyword abilities." Mitchell Cimino character card has the response "After you spend 1 or more momentum, remove them. Add the cards removed this way to your hand at the start of the End Phase." Due to Spellstorm's effect, the game only treats the flipped cards as spending momentum for the explicit purposes of paying the cost of keyword abilities. It does not change the act of flipping Spell cards in the card pool into spending momentum for all game references. Mitchell Cimino's response ability is looking for cards to actually be spent from momentum.
bullets: If an effect allows a player to pay an alternate cost to play an ability, it does not change the activity of the cost to the normal activity.

name: Asset
definition:
Assets are one of the six card types.

You play assets as a form into the card pool during the Combat Phase, and they get built into the stage during the end phase. (just like foundations)

Unlike foundations, you can't commit assets to pass checks.

If a card counts or refers to your assets, it means assets in your stage.

name: Assigned Effect
definition:
Assigned effects are one of the three effect states. (An effect's "state" dictates how it behaves if related cards move or change. see: Effect States)

Any non-static effect that is specific to a particular card, is an assigned effect.

Assigned effects end when:

This ability creates an assigned effect on the chosen foundation. It will end when the Enhance Step ends, or if the chosen foundation changes zones.)
examples: Natural Leader has the ability "First Enhance: Choose 1 rival foundation. Abilities on the chosen card can not be played for the rest of the Enhance Step."
notes: assigned effects do not end if the card flips or unflips.
bullets: They finish executing, or can no longer do so.
A point specified by the effect is reached.
The turn ends.
The card the effect is assigned to changes zones.

name: Attack
definition:
Attacks are one of the six card types.

You play attacks as a form into the card pool during the Combat Phase. They either go to momentum (if they deal damage) or get discarded during the End Phase.

Attacks have three unique properties - speed, damage and attack zone. These can't be modified unless the attack is inside of its attack sequence, or just got played and is about to enter one.

Enhance abilities on an attack are playable during that attack's Enhance Step.

Response abilities on attacks are playable during that attack's attack sequence.

"During an attack" means to be during the attack sequence.

A player or backup is considered attacked after the attack has been played.

Abilities that respond to the attack being played (which trigger before the attack sequence starts) will be usable. Anything that triggers on the attack resolving will also be usable.)

"This attack" on a non-attack card, or an enhance ability, means the attack currently in progress (in its attack sequence, being played, was just played, or just resolved).

In a continuous or response ability on an attack, "This attack" only refers to the attack the ability is on.

"Your attack" or "Your rival's attack" (in any scenario) refer to the attack in progress, provided it is either yours or your rival's, respectively.
examples: Erase's Response says "your rival cannot play abilities on their character during this attack." This means that they can't play abilities on their character during the Enhance Step, Block Step or Damage Step.
notes: Universus CCG does not have "targeting". This means you are free to play "your attack" abilities during your rival's attack, even if the effect would fail to do anything.
bullets: You may choose to discard an attack that dealt damage during the End Phase, if you don't want the momentum.
If an attack is in the process of being played, instead of a modification failing to occur, it happens once the attack is played (if successful).
For attacks added to the attack stack that are not played, any relevant pending continuous or fired effects will apply once the attack enters the attack sequence.

name: Attack Sequence
definition:
The attack sequence starts after you play an attack.

Note that any fired effects or responses to playing an attack happen before the attack sequence begins.

The attack sequence contains the Enhance Step, Block Step, and Damage Step (in that order).

If you are attacking a backup, skip the Block Step and proceed from the Enhance Step to the Damage Step immediately.

After the Damage Step, the attack is resolved.

If an attack leaves the card pool or changes facing during its attack sequence, but before the Damage Step, it becomes a dropped attack.
bullets: Dropped attacks end their attack sequence at the next opportunity. (see: Dropped Attack)

name: Attack Stack
definition:
Whenever an attack is played, but not as a cost, it is added to an abstract list called the "attack stack".

Note: This is not a game zone. It has no impact on play other than keeping track of which attacks occur in which order should there be multiple attacks pending at once.

Some effects (like Beauregard Lionett, Expositor's enhance ability) create attacks during a current attack. Attacks created in the middle of a current attack are added to the Attack Stack. These attacks are not considered played.
bullets: There is only one attack stack, shared by both players.
A card cannot be added to the attack stack unless it is an attack in the card pool.
Attacks on the attack stack are called "pending attacks".
Whenever a form could otherwise be played, (points 1 through 3 are all true) enter an attack sequence for the pending attack most recently added to the attack stack, and remove it from the stack. 7
If a pending attack leaves the card pool or gets turned face down, remove it from the attack stack.

If more than one attack is to be added to the attack stack at the same time, add them starting with the rightmost attack in the card pool and moving left. Note: This means that when that group of attacks begin to initiate attack sequences, they will occur in order from left to right, since the leftmost attack was added last.

name: Attack Zone
definition: Indicated by the arrowed circle on the right side of an attack. A highlighted top arrow means it's a high attack, the middle arrow means it's a mid attack, and the bottom arrow means it's a low attack.
bullets: High attacks are completely blocked by high blocks, and partially blocked by mid blocks. They can't be blocked with low blocks.
Mid attacks are completely blocked by mid blocks, and partially blocked by high or low blocks.
Low attacks are completely blocked by low blocks, and partially blocked by mid blocks. They can't be blocked with high blocks.

name: Attacker
definition: The player whose attack is currently in its attack sequence. The attacker gets the first chance to play enhance abilities during the Enhance Step.

name: Attuned Symbols
definition:
Some cards have resource symbols with a square icon, rather than a circular symbol icon. This indicates that that symbol is attuned. A card with an attuned symbol on it can be played in a deck whose starting character possesses the corresponding resource symbol regardless of the deck's primary symbol. (The character does not need to possess an attuned symbol on their card, just the base symbol. For example, Eren Jaeger possesses the symbol. So even if his deck's primary symbol is , he can play an attuned card such as Spring into Action.)

🔥

💀

🔥

If a card has multiple attuned symbols, your starting character must possess one of the attuned symbols, but does not require both.

name: Backup
definition:
Backups are one of the six card types.

You play backups into the card pool as a form during the Combat Phase, and they get built into the stage during the end phase (just like foundations).

Unlike foundations, you can't commit backups to pass checks. If a card counts or refers to your backups, it means the backups in your stage.

Backups have 1 unique property—stamina.

When playing an attack, the attacking player may choose to attack a backup in the rival's stage rather than the rival character. When their backup is being attacked, the defending player cannot try to play a block. (see: Attack Sequence)
bullets: Stamina is the number over the green heart icon. (see: Stamina)

name: Blitz Ability
definition:
Blitz abilities are played during the start of the Enhance Step, before Enhance abilities are played. Once a player has finished playing Blitz abilities, they may play an Enhance ability. They may not play Blitz abilities after the start of the Enhance Step.

(see: Enhance Step)

A blitz ability is playable only if it's on the current attack.

Each blitz ability can only be played once per Enhance Step.
bullets: Blitz abilities are optional.
When a card has more than one Blitz ability, you may play any number of those Blitz abilities in any order you choose.

name: Block
definition:
Indicated by the shield icon on the top right corner of a card.

Blocks have two parts. A zone, marked by the location of the "+ marking" next to the shield, and a modifier number.

"Play a block" triggers react to playing a card as a block, not just playing any card that has a block.
bullets: Playing a card as a block does not count as "playing a [card type]". In other words, blocking with an attack is not playing an attack.
Playing a block is still "playing a card", or "playing a [card type] card".

name: Block Modifier
definition:
The block modifier is the number inside the block icon.

When you try to block, the difficulty you check against is equal to: The speed of the attack + the

block modifier of your block + progressive difficulty. (see: Progressive Difficulty)

name: Block Step
definition:
The second part of the attack sequence. After the Enhance Step, move on to the Block Step. The Block Step has two parts.

First, the defender decides if they want to try to block. They can't try to block unless they have a card in their hand that's valid to block with.

If the zone of the attack is:

If they chose to try to block, they choose a valid card and try to play it as a block.

Example - Tape Swing cannot be completely blocked. Because of this, any block with a matching zone is invalid.)

The difficulty you check against to block with a card is: The speed of the attack + the block modifier of the block + progressive difficulty.

Second, determine how and whether the attack was blocked.

"Attack is not blocked / completely blocked / partially blocked" triggers happen at this time.

After the Block Step, move to the Damage Step.
notes: "Valid" means a card that can block the attack. There may be effects that change what blocks are valid.

The block is played first, and any responses or fired effects to the card being played as a block happen. Then the attack becomes blocked, and any responses or fired effects to that happen. ) An attack can only have one such "block status". If an attack's block status changes after being initially determined, triggers to blocking do not happen again.
bullets: If an effect makes an attack "considered completely blocked during the Block Step", skip the first part of the Block Step. The attack becomes completely blocked during the second part.

The attacking player's effects cannot create a "blocked" status for their own attacks. Any instruction to do so fails. This means that the effect occurs, but the blocked status does not take effect and the block step proceeds as normal.
A player cannot try to block if the rival attacks one of their backup cards.
High - blocks with a high or mid zone are valid
Mid - all block zones are valid
Low - blocks with a low or mid zone are valid
If a block becomes invalid before it is done being played, abort the process of playing a block and discard the block. You don't get to try to block again.
If the defender did not try to block, or failed the check to play their block, the attack is not blocked.
If the defender played a block with a block zone that matches the attack zone, the attack is completely blocked.
If the defender played a block with a block zone that does not match the attack zone, the attack is partially blocked.

name: Block Zone
definition: The block zone is at the right side of the block icon. A "+ marking" at the top means it's a high block, in the middle means it's a mid block, and at the bottom means it's a low block.
bullets: A high block can completely block a high attack, and partially block a mid attack.
A mid block can completely block a mid attack, and partially block a high or low attack.
A low block can completely block a low attack, and partially block a mid attack.

name: Bonus
definition:
A bonus is a +# increase to some number.

If a number increases via some means other than giving it +#, that is not a bonus.
examples: "This attack gets +2 speed" gives an attack a speed bonus. "Your check gets +1" is a check bonus. "Your attack gets a damage bonus equal to its printed difficulty" will give a printed 5 difficulty attack +5 damage.
If you use Encouraging Training Partner to make your 0 speed attack into a 4 speed attack, that is not a speed bonus.

name: Both Players
definition:
Some effects have both players do something at the same time, but they need to make a choice (i.e. choose a card) to do so. The owner of the effect may choose who makes the decision first. Aside from any such decisions, the effect still occurs simultaneously.

You can ask your rival to choose their card (and whether to reveal at all) first, but the cards are only revealed once both players have chosen.)

Similar to Flex Your Might, players will choose their cards first (you can ask your rival to choose before you if you'd like), then both players discard the cards at the same time and then draw 2 cards.)

You may ask your rival to choose what they want to destroy first, before you do. All four foundations are destroyed at the same time.)
examples: You play the form on Flex Your Might, which makes both players reveal a card at the same time.
You play the enhance on Petty Squabble, which makes both players discard 2 cards.
You play the combo enhance on Gigantic Explosion, which has both players destroy 2 of their rival's foundations.

name: Brackets
definition:
Brackets after the ability type restrict how that ability is played.

If brackets contain a game zone, it means that ability is only playable when the card is in that zone. It also means the ability can be played from that zone, if it normally couldn't.

Brackets can also contain a timing (such as "[Your attack]"), which means the ability can only be played during that time.

Lastly, brackets can have a count restriction, such as "[Once per turn]" or "[Once per game]". That means you can only play the ability that many times per time span.

name: Breaker
definition:
Breaker is a keyword ability.

Breaker grants "Response: After you block with this card, your rival's next check to play a card gets -X. X equals the rating of the Breaker keyword granting this ability."

The Breaker rating is the number next to the Breaker keyword. "Breaker: 2" has a Breaker rating of 2.

name: Build
definition:
To "build" a card means to add it to your stage.

If the effect doesn't specify facing, face down cards from zones other than the hand build face down. Anything else builds face up.

If by some means you build an attack or action face up, discard it immediately. (see: Pause Conditions)

name: Bullets in Abilities
definition:
Some abilities instruct you or a rival to choose from a bulleted list of effects. Such an ability cannot execute until all costs have been paid and all choices required by the ability have been made. Once those criteria are satisfied, the ability is considered to have been played, and its effects are only those effects which were chosen. Choosing an option is neither a cost, nor an effect

Unless an ability specifically allows it, choices on a bulleted list of effects cannot be chosen more than once.

If an option on a bulleted list is not chosen as part of the effect, other card effects will not consider it to be an [Ability That ____]. This exception applies only to abilities with a list of bulleted options. (see: Ability That ____)
examples: Player A plays the Enhance ability on "Wielding One For All" with 5 Vestige cards in their stage, allowing for 2 choices on the list of effects. When playing the card, they pay the cost of removing the card from play. Then, they make the choice between the options listed, choosing to draw 1 card twice. Once the choice has been made the effects are set and will then execute.

Player A plays the attack "Shoot the Moss" and uses the Blitz ability, causing Player B to choose between granting the attack a speed bonus or a damage bonus. There is no cost, so Player B moves to making the choice between the effects, choosing to give a damage bonus. Player A responds to that choice with the response on "Twin Twains", flipping the card and causing Player B to choose the remaining choice of giving a speed bonus. Since both choices have been made, the effects are set and will then execute.
notes: This means that in the above example using "Wielding One For All", the played ability will not count as an ability that increases speed or provides a damage bonus.

name: Cancel
definition:
An effect that cancels an ability prevents that ability's effect(s) from executing.

An ability that has been cancelled has still been played.

Because costs are paid before an ability is played, cancelling an ability does not reverse its cost being paid.

name: Card Pool
definition:
The card pool is one of the game zones.

When you play a card, add it to the card pool.

If the card (such as Erase!) goes to another zone as part of its cost, it does not go to the card pool.

Add cards to the card pool left to right. The order of cards in the card pool can't be changed.

Each card in the card pool adds 1 to the progressive difficulty to play other cards.

The card pool is just a sequence of cards, left to right, with new cards being added at the end. If a card leaves your card pool, slide any cards after it over to the left to help keep this clear.)

Face down cards in the card pool are blank cards with no card type or other properties.

If a face down card goes to the card pool (except from the hand), it stays face down by default.

Some effects look for the preceding card or card(s) to a referenced card. These look to the left of the referenced card. (see: Preceding)

The card pool empties during the End Phase. (see: Clearing the Card Pool)
notes: If a card leaves the card pool, it no longer adds to progressive difficulty
If you're using a playmat with defined spaces for cards in the card pool, these are not individual "slots". They're just an aid to help see progressive difficulty.

name: Character
definition:
Characters are one of the six card types.

Characters have four unique properties - hand size, health, identity and version.

Hand size is the number over the 'cards' icon.

Health is the number over the 'heart' icon.

Identity is the name at the top of the card, excluding parentheses.

Version is the roman numeral to the top right of the text box (see: Hand Size, Health, Identity, Version)

The full card name of a character card is the name at the top, plus the version.

(see: Making a Check, Costs)

have the same identity and version, it gets discarded instead.

Whenever another copy of your starting character enters your stage, it attaches to your starting character.

Attached characters, aside from effects that count the number of characters / cards in the stage, are "merged into" the starting character.
examples: : The full card name of the Tenya Iida that comes in DLC pack 1 is "Tenya Iida 2". The full card name of All Might (True Form) is "All Might (True Form) 4".
Mt. Lady can add other copies of Mt. Lady to her stage. When she does, the added copy attaches to the original. Aside from counting for her second ability, these added copies don't do anything.
notes: This only applies to *costs*, not effects like that of Stun.
see "Place On Top Of" for how to handle All Might (True Form)'s once per game ability.
bullets: This is for the purposes of naming a card, deck registration, etc.
If an effect only mentions a character identity but no version, any character card with that identity counts.
You can commit your character to help pass control checks.
The character you start the game with in your stage is your starting character.
The starting character can't be destroyed, flipped, or otherwise removed from the stage.
If an effect refers to your character, it means your starting character.
If you play a copy of your starting character as a form, it builds when it gets cleared during the End Phase.
If you play a character that's not a copy of your starting character - as in, it does not
They don't commit or ready on their own. They have the same committed/ready state as your starting character.
They don't count for anything when passing checks.
You can't play their abilities.
They can't be used for a cost, or impacted by an effect, unless it (explicitly or implicitly) calls for attached characters.

name: Character Abilities
definition: Character abilities are abilities on a character card. If an effect references character abilities, it only references abilities that originate from a character card.

name: Character Traits
definition:
Characters may have Trait keywords.

Character traits have no inherent function, but cards may reference them.

Character traits may not be added to non-character cards through any means.

The following is a list of current Character Traits:

1-A 1-B Big 3 Brawler Champion E-Rank Ensign Garrison Giant Healer Hunter Inventor League of Villains Lurkers Mighty Nein Military Police Brigade Monster Nomu Pirate Pro Hero Scout Shie Hassaikai Shiketsu Student Survey Corps Teacher Team Masho Team Rokuyokai Team Toguro Team Urameshi Team Uraotogi Thief Titan Villain Vox Machina Warrior

name: Charge
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Check
definition: see Making a check

name: Check Value
definition:
The check value of a card is the number in the lower right corner.

When you make a check (see: Making a Check), this is the number that you'll compare to the difficulty you need.

A check value surrounded by a burst visual effect is a reminder that the card has a response ability that triggers when checked.

name: Clear
definition: To clear a card means to move it from your card pool to your discard pile.
bullets: If an effect is looking for a card to have been discarded from the card pool, a cleared card counts, and vice versa.
Cards that are put anywhere but the discard pile from the card pool are not cleared.

name: Clearing the Card Pool
definition:
Both players clear their card pool during the End Phase. The active player does so first, then the inactive player.

Clear your card pool right to left, so the first card you play is the last one to clear.

When cleared:
bullets: Action cards, cards played as blocks, face down cards, and cards in the card pool that were not played, get discarded.
Foundations (that you played as foundations) get built to your Stage.
Assets (that you played as assets) get built to your Stage.
Attacks that dealt damage may (your choice) go to your momentum. If you don't want it to or if it didn't deal damage, it gets discarded.
Characters get discarded, unless it's a copy of your starting character, in which case you build and attach it. (see: Character)

name: Combat Phase
definition:
The Combat Phase is the main phase of the game. It is the second part of a turn, between the Start Phase and End Phase.

During the Combat Phase, the active player takes a series of actions that are collectively referred to as "forms".

A form can be:

If you play an attack, begin the Attack Sequence (see: Attack Sequence).

As the active player, you have priority to play a form (or pass) whenever:

If you try to play a card as a form, but fail the check to play it, the Combat Phase ends.

You must try to play at least one card as a form during your Combat Phase.
bullets: Playing an attack, foundation, asset or character (see: Playing a card)
Playing a form ability on an action card
Playing a form ability on a card in the stage
You can also pass on playing a form, and move to the End Phase. (This is not a form)
There is no ongoing attack sequence
There are no response windows open
There are no cards or abilities in the middle of being played or executing
This includes playing a form ability on an action card.
If you can't, reveal your hand to verify that none of the cards can be played as forms. Then discard 1 card.

name: Combo
definition:
Combo is a keyword ability, and also an associated play restriction. (see: Play Restrictions)

The Combo keyword will always be followed by some requirement(s) in parentheses. For example, Combo (Ranged) or Combo (Weapon, Foundation).

The Combo keyword grants:

The Combo play restriction makes an ability only playable if the combo requirements are met.

If a Combo keyword has multiple requirements, those requirements will be separated by a comma.

If the Charge attack is not *directly* before Improvised Blade (i.e. if it's three cards back), that won't meet the requirement. If the card directly before is not a Charge as printed, but gained Charge from an effect, this does not work either.)

Staff Strike are a card that has Weapon as a printed keyword, followed by a Foundation card.

If the order of cards in your card pool is not exactly Weapon card, Foundation, Staff Strike, that won't meet the requirement.)

If an effect references a Combo ability, it's looking for an ability with a Combo play restriction.

Face Down cards in your card pool may fulfill the requirement for Combo (Face Down), but no other combo restriction.
examples: Improvised Blade has Combo (Charge) and a Combo Enhance that gives it more damage. You can only play that enhance if the card directly before Improvised Blade has Charge as a printed keyword.

Staff Strike has Combo (Weapon, Foundation) and a Combo Enhance that makes the next two Weapon cards you try to play this turn ignore progressive difficulty. You can only play that enhance if the two cards directly before
notes: The "printed" here is important! The card(s) before your combo attack must match the requirement as printed. Changes to cards due to effects have no bearing on whether you can play a combo ability.
bullets: "Combo-restricted abilities on this card are only playable if the printed properties of the directly preceding card(s) in the card pool match the requirement(s) of a Combo keyword on this card, in order."

name: Commit
definition:
A card can be committed by using it to pass a check, to pay some cost, or due to some effect.

To show that a card has been committed, turn it 90 degrees clockwise.

A committed card cannot be committed.

You can't play abilities on a committed card.

Only cards in the stage can be ready or committed.

When the following icon appears on a card (usually as part of the cost of an ability), it means "commit this card."
bullets: Unless the ability has [Tenacious] (see: Tenacious)
Continuous abilities on committed cards remain in effect.

name: Completely Blocked
definition:
If an attack is blocked by a card with the same block zone as the attack's attack zone, the attack is completely blocked. (see: Block Step)

Completely blocked attacks do not deal damage in the Damage Step.
notes: Throw attacks can be completely blocked, even though it still results in damage.

name: Conflict Resolution
definition:
If an effect directly conflicts with the game rules, the effect takes precedence.

It does not mean you get to play a card that would be illegal for some other reason. And if it did not specify that a check isn't necessary, you would still need to make one and pass it.)

Effects that prohibit / prevent, take precedence in any conflict.
examples: You use the second enhance on Electric Jolt, which makes it deal 1 damage when completely blocked. Your rival uses Wall Climb to say it deals no damage when blocked. Wall Climb takes precedence no matter what order the two were used in.
notes: This is not "greedy". For example, Mina Ashido lets you play a card from your discard pile as a form, with no check necessary. This only overrides (a) you normally can't play cards out of your discard pile, and (b) the need to pass a check to play said card.

name: Continuous Ability
definition:
A continuous ability is an ability that is not played. Instead, it gives the card a static effect. (see: Static Effects)

If an ability does not have a type designation (Enhance, Form or Response), it is a continuous ability.
examples: several cards have the ability "If this attack is added to your momentum, add it face up." This is a continuous ability.
bullets: If a continuous ability is preceded by a set icon or symbol restriction, it only grants the effect if your starting character has that set icon or symbol.

name: Copy
definition:
A copy of a card is a card with the same name.

A copy of an ability is an ability with the same cost and effect, on a copy of the same card.
bullets: Cards with no name are not copies of each other.

name: Copying Another Card
definition:
Some effects turn a card into a copy of another card, or allow you to play a card as a copy of another card.

These effects are a special type of modifier effect, which are applied before any other modifier effect(s).

Copying a card copies every property of the card - its name, its card type, block modifier, keywords, abilities, etc.

name: Costs
definition:
The cost of an ability is the instruction(s), if any, before the colon. It comes after the type designation and any restrictions / brackets.

You can't try to play an ability that you can't pay the cost for.

When paying a cost, all triggers wait until the cost is done being paid. This means that nothing can interrupt and change your ability to pay the cost.

If a cost includes X, then either:

Some commonly used costs have shorthanded terms:

If a cost asks you to interact with card(s), it means your own cards by default. (see: Instructions)
examples: The Attack Titan is in your stage and currently has 3 stamina. You cannot lose 4 stamina to pay for its form ability.
"Discard 1 momentum" means to discard 1 of your own momentum. Tsuyu Asui 2's "Discard 1 card from your rival's card pool" is paid by choosing a rival card to discard, since it specifies as such.
bullets: This excludes the check cost to play an action.
X is defined at the end of the effect's text.
If it's not specified, you can choose any non-negative value for X when you announce that you're playing the ability.
"Commit" - "Commit this card"
"Destroy" - "Destroy this card"
"Remove" - "Remove this card from the game"
"Flip" - "Turn this card face down"
"Check X" - "Make a check against a difficulty of X"

name: Counters
definition:
Some game effects may generate counters to be added to a card. Usually, these counters will be distinctly named. Counters with different names should be tracked separately.

By default, counters cannot be on cards unless they are in the Stage.
bullets: If an effect calls out a specific type of counter, it is looking for ONLY that type of counter.
If an effect refers to a counter or counters on a card without specifying a type of counter, that effect refers to any and all counters on that card.

name: Current State
definition:
The current state of a card is the properties that it has right now.

In the Universus CCG, a card's current state changes as you apply effects, and those effects get consumed.

It is *not* a 3 speed, 4 damage attack with "+1 speed", "-3 speed" and "+2 damage" effects floating on it in some way.

As an example where this matters, say you use "Encouraging Training Partner" to change the attack's speed to 4. This turns your 1 speed, 6 damage attack into a 4 speed, 6 damage attack, full stop.

You do not go back down the list of +/- effects to recalculate starting from 4. Those effects are already done and gone. Only the current state of the card's properties matters.)

Cards get a current state as soon as they become live (see: Live Cards). If you try to play a card that was already live, it gets a new current state as soon as the attempt begins.

Current states are also refreshed at the end of a turn, or when a card changes zones or facing (face up / face down).

When a card gets a new or refreshed current state, it starts at its printed properties, then modifier effects get applied (see: Modifier Effects).

Once an effect has applied a change to a card's current state, that modification can't get undone, repeated, or otherwise changed until the current state is refreshed or replaced.

(a) Todoroki gives your attack +1 damage for every 2 Ranged attacks in your discard pile. You use this ability with 9 Ranged attacks in your discard pile, so the attack gets +4 damage.

Later on in the attack, you get a 10th ranged attack in your discard. This doesn't matter though, because the +4 is already applied and done. It does not become +5.

(b) Giant Ice Wall can give your next Ranged attack Stun: 2. You play another Ranged attack, and it gains Stun: 2. Even if it loses the Ranged keyword for some reason, it will not lose the Stun: 2.)

Current state creation / refreshes happen before any fired effects can trigger.

While an attack is being played, any changes to its speed, damage or attack zone are suspended until it finishes getting played.

The speed, zone and damage of an attack can't be changed if it is not being played or in its attack sequence.
examples: You have a 3 speed, 4 damage attack, and it gets +1 speed, +2 damage, and -3 speed from a series of effects. It is now a 1 speed, 6 damage attack, and those +/- effects are done and over.

name: Cycling
definition:
Cycling is a pause condition (see: Pause Conditions) that occurs when a player has no cards in their deck and the last card to leave their deck has reached its destination.

To cycle your deck, shuffle your discard pile to make a new deck, then remove the top 10 cards of that new deck from the game.

You may need to draw, discard or otherwise take more cards off of your deck than it has left. If this happens, take as many as you can, then cycle, then take the rest.
bullets: If you don't have 10 cards to remove, you lose the game.
Note that this does not apply to revealing cards, since those cards aren't leaving your deck.

name: Damage
definition:
The number in the burst icon of an attack is its damage.

Damage can be negative (see: Negative Numbers). Attacks do not deal negative damage - if an attack would deal negative damage, it does not deal damage.

When a player takes damage, they lose that much health.

name: Damage Step
definition:
The third part of the attack sequence, after the Block Step. During the Damage Step, the attack deals (or fails to deal) damage to the rival or a backup card in their stage.

After the Damage Step, the attack resolves and the attack sequence ends.
bullets: Unblocked attacks deal damage equal to their damage value.
Partially blocked attacks deal half their damage value, rounded up.
Completely blocked attacks deal no damage.

name: Deadlock
definition:
Deadlock is a play restriction. (see: Play Restrictions)

Deadlock abilities are only playable if your rival has more than 10 (as in, 11 or more) foundations in their stage. This only counts foundations, not characters or assets.
bullets: If an effect checks to see if a player is "at deadlock" or "in deadlock", it is checking to see if they have more than 10 foundations.

name: Deals Damage
definition: An attack must deal 1 or more damage to count as dealing damage. If an attack would "deal" 0 or negative damage, it does not deal damage.

name: Deck
definition:
Your deck is the stack of cards you draw and use for checks over the course of the game.

The number of cards in each player's deck is public knowledge.

If you run out cards in your deck, cycle it. (see: Cycling)

If multiple cards are added to your deck at the same time, you can choose their order.

If you search your deck due to some effect, shuffle it afterwards. If the effect only lets you search for a certain kind of card, you need to reveal it to verify that you met the conditions.

name: Deck Construction
definition:
These rules apply to the UniVersus Standard format. For other formats, see their relevant tournament documents.

A deck contains a minimum of 60 cards, plus 1 character.

(You cannot include characters without a printed difficulty or check value in your deck. They may only be used as a starting character.)

(tip: Using a deck with more than the minimum 60 cards can be a good idea in the Universus CCG, so keep that in mind!)

In tournament play, you can also bring exactly 10 cards to form a sideboard. A sideboard lets you substitute cards between games. Your deck

must have the same number of cards after sideboarding that it did before.

No more than four copies of a given card can be in your deck, including your sideboard.

Choose 1 of your character's resource symbols to be your deck's primary symbol. Every card in your deck must possess the deck's primary symbol. Attuned symbol cards may break this rule. (See: Attuned Symbols)

An ability that grants a card's text to another card, or temporarily turns 1 card into a copy of another card will allow a player to exceed the maximum number of a particular card in their deck.

Example: A Toya Setsuno player included 8 copies of Resounding Screech in their deck and used their Form ability to play a face down card as a copy of Resounding Screech. This would technically bring their total copies to 9, but will not cause an error because the deck construction restriction is only checked at the start of a game.
examples: Say you want to build a deck with Momo Yaoyorozu as your starting character. It must be entirely All symbol cards, entirely Good symbol cards, or entirely Life symbol cards. This includes the sideboard.
bullets: The 1 character is your designated starting character. You can include other characters in your deck for blocking or other reasons as long as they have difficulty and check values.

name: Defender
definition: The rival of the player whose attack is currently in its attack sequence. The defender gets priority to play Enhance abilities second during the Enhance Step.

name: Deflect
definition:
Deflect is a keyword ability.

Deflect grants "Response [Hand] Add this card to the card pool: Before the damage step of a rival attack, that attack gets -X damage. X equals the rating of the Deflect keyword granting this ability. Only 1 Deflect ability can be played per attack."

NOTE: Deflect modifies damage of the attack before damage is calculated for a partial block.

name: Desperation (definition)
definition:
Desperation is both a play restriction and a keyword ability.

Desperation abilities are only playable if your current health is less than half of your maximum health.
examples: : A character with 30 maximum health is at desperation when their current health is 14. They would NOT be at desperation when their current health is at 15, as that is not less than half of their maximum health.
bullets: If an effect checks to see if a player is "at desperation", it is checking to see if they have less than half their maximum health.

name: Desperation (Keyword Ability)
definition: If Desperation is presented as a keyword ability with a rating (example: Desperation: 3 on Mop Strike) then that Keyword grants the ability "While you are at desperation, this card's difficulty is X. X equals the rating of the Desperation keyword granting this ability."
examples: If your character has a maximum health of 27 and your current health is 13, when you try to play Sword Get Longer its Desperation: 4 ability will change the difficulty of the card from 6 to 4 before any other modifications to determine the difficulty to play the card.

name: Destroy
definition: To destroy a card means to move it from the stage to the discard pile.

name: Detached Effect
definition:
Detached effects are one of the three effect states. (An effect's "state" dictates how it behaves if related cards move or change. see: Effect States)

Detached effects are completely independent of the state of any card(s). Any non-static effect that is not specific to a particular card(s) is detached. This includes all direct effects. (see: Direct Effects)

A detached effect ends when either: It finishes executing A point specified in the effect is reached The turn ends (if no other end point is specified)
bullets: For a fired effect, when all possible chances for it to trigger have passed

name: Difficulty
definition:
The difficulty of a card is the number in the top left corner.

The difficulty you check against is separate. That is the number that your check must meet or exceed in order to pass a check.

When you play a card (aside from blocking), the difficulty you check against is equal to the card's difficulty, plus progressive difficulty.

When you try to block with a card, the difficulty you check against is equal to: The speed of the attack + the block modifier of your block + progressive difficulty.

(see: Progressive Difficulty)

name: Direct Effect
definition:
Direct effects are one of the four effect classes. (An effect's "class" dictates how or when it is applied, among other things)

A direct effect is the effect of a played ability. It executes after the ability is played.

Direct effects are always detached. They often create other effects, including assigned effects.
bullets: The direct effect of Note Taking gives your punch attack +2 speed.
The direct effect of Reverse Throw creates an assigned fired effect.)

name: Discard
definition:
To discard a card is to put it in your discard pile. This can be from any zone.

In card text, if no zone is specified, "discard a card" means to discard it from your hand.

If the cost of an ability is to discard your hand, you can pay that cost with an empty hand.
bullets: Similarly, if card text references a card being discarded, or some like extrapolation of the above, it means discarded from hand unless otherwise specified.
If the cost of an ability is to discard a certain number of cards, you must be able to discard that many cards to pay the cost.

name: Discard Pile
definition:
The discard pile contains cards that were destroyed, checked or otherwise discarded.

Your discard pile is a single face up pile, with new cards going on top. You can't rearrange it, and both players are allowed to look at either discard pile at any time.

If multiple cards go to your discard pile at the same time, you can choose the order they go in.

name: Double
definition: Effects cannot double a negative number.

name: Double-Sided Cards
definition:
A Double-Sided card is a UniVersus card that has no "UniVersus" card back. Instead, it has a front side and a back side.

The front side of a double-sided card has normal symbol icons, a difficulty value, and a check value.
bullets: Double-sided character cards may or may not have difficulty or check values. The back side of a card does not have a difficulty or check value. Additionally, it has photonegative versions of the front side's set identifier and resource symbols to make it easy to identify which side is the back side.

name: Draw
definition:
To draw a card means to add the top card of your deck to your hand.

Drawing multiple cards is one game event. (Effects that trigger off of drawing cards will only trigger once)

If an effect asks you to "draw up to" a certain number, and you have less than that, draw the difference in one action.
bullets: Adding cards to your hand in other ways does not count as drawing a card.

name: Draw Step
definition:
The Draw Step is the third part of the Start Phase, after the Review Step.

During your draw step, draw up to your hand size.

If the number of cards in your hand is already equal to or more than your hand size, don't draw any cards.

name: Drive
definition: To "drive X" is to follow the instruction "commit foundations and/or spend momentum in any combination of X."

name: Dropped Attack
definition:
If an attack in its attack sequence leaves the card pool or changes facing (face up or face down) before the Damage Step, it becomes dropped.

Dropped attacks end their attack sequence as soon as either:
bullets: A player would get priority to play an enhance
The attack sequence would move on to the start of a new step, or to the determination of block status. (see: Attack Sequence)

name: During an Attack
definition: "During an attack" means to be during an attack sequence.
notes: effects that trigger upon an attack being played happen before the attack sequence

name: Echo
definition:
Echo is a keyword ability.

Echo grants "Response Discard 1 momentum: After this attack resolves, you may play it as your next form. If you do, after it resolves, flip it."

The flipped card does not count as an attack and will not go to momentum.

An Echo ability on a face down card cannot be played.

name: Effects
definition:
An effect is some series of instructions that can have an impact on the game. Effects come from abilities, or from other effects.

Execute effects' instructions in order. Each step along the way can separately trigger fired effects or response abilities.

If an effect requires a decision, that decision is not made until that part of the effect is reached.

Effects do as much as they can, even if it is not possible to do the whole effect.

If an effect asks a player to interact with cards, it means their own cards by default. (see: Instructions)

Certain clauses of an effect are handled out of order:

There can be text at the end of an effect that impacts the ability and is not part of the effect. These can include:

"Playable while committed." "Only playable (condition)." "This ability cannot be canceled." "This enhance is playable any number of times this Enhance Step."

(see: Play Restrictions)

Some effects are a series of bullets. These function like a list of options, except that you will need to choose an option as part of the cost.

(see: Bulleted Effects)

Effects have both a "class" and a "state".

Non-static effects last until the end of the turn, if no other end point is specified.

Sometimes an effect looks for a card property, but the card changed zones or visibility. Use the latest value of that property in the first zone where it was live. (see: Scope)
examples: "Your rival commits 1 foundation" means they pick and commit 1 of their own foundations. "Destroy 1 rival foundation" means you pick and destroy any rival foundation, since it specifies as such.
notes: If an effect makes another effect that includes a decision, the decision isn't made until that created effect executes.
It is possible for an effect to do nothing at all. There is nothing wrong with this, and you're allowed to play an ability even if its effect will do nothing.
Effects can also have a "scope", which limits an effect's frame of reference for certain things. It's mostly a technicality, but you can see Scope for details.
bullets: Game procedures, such as cycling, are not effects.
Fired effects or response abilities can interrupt an effect. Handle those, and any resulting triggers, before continuing the original effect.
Acting on groups of cards (i.e. discard 2 cards) does not cause multiple triggers. (see: Game Event)
A definition of X may appear in an effect, but is not an instruction.
An "If... instead" clause changes a prior part of the effect based on a condition.
An "Either… or if" clause is an alternate way to frame the above.
"Your rival may (instructions) to cancel this ability."
"This effect cannot ___."
An effect's "class" dictates how or when it is applied, among other things. (see: Effect Classes)
An effect's "state" dictates how it behaves if related cards move or change. (see: Effect States)

name: Effect Classes
definition:
The class of an effect dictates how it is applied, its timing, scope and any unique behaviors.

A direct effect is the effect of a played ability. (see: Direct Effect)

A fired effect is triggered by some game event, like a response ability, but not optional. (see: Fired Effect)

A modifier effect changes a card's current state. They can happen immediately, or wait for card(s) to apply to.

(see: Current State, Modifier Effect)

A rule effect applies some added rule or behavior to the game while it's active. (see: Rule Effect)

name: Effect States
definition:
An effect's "state" dictates how it behaves if related cards move or change.

Static effects are active so long as the card they're on is live. (see: Static Effect, Continuous Ability, Live Card)

Detached effects are completely independent of the state of any card(s). (see: Detached Effect)

Assigned effects are tied to a specific card, and will end if that card changes zones. (see: Assigned Effect)

name: Either Player
definition: If an effect instructs you to interact with "either player's" game zone(s), you pick a player and interact with that one player's game zone(s).

name: Elusive
definition:
Elusive is a keyword ability.

Elusive grants "This backup cannot be attacked by a rival's first attack each turn."

name: End Phase
definition:
The End Phase is the third part of the turn, and comes after the Combat Phase.

During the End Phase, players clear their card pools, then the turn ends and the other player's turn begins. (see: Clearing the Card Pool)

name: Enhance Ability
definition:
Enhance abilities are played during the Enhance Step. (see: Enhance Step)

An enhance ability is playable if it's on a ready card in the stage, an action card in the hand, or on the current attack.

Each enhance ability can only be played once per Enhance Step.

name: Enhance Step
definition:
The Enhance Step is the first part of the attack sequence, before the Block Step.

At the start of the Enhance Step, the active player may play any blitz abilities they have access to. Then the Enhance step continues with each player taking turns having priority to play Enhance abilities. (see: Enhance Ability)

When you have priority, you can either play an Enhance, or pass.

If both players pass in succession, the Enhance Step ends.
bullets: Passing does not stop you from playing Enhances later on. You only pass that specific chance to play one.

name: EX
definition:
EX is a keyword ability.

EX grants "Enhance Discard 1 or more momentum: This attack gets +X speed for each card used to pay the cost of this ability. X equals the rating of the EX keyword granting this ability."

The EX rating is the number next to the EX keyword. "EX: 2" has a

EX rating of 2.

name: Face Down Attacks
definition: Some effects may allow you to have a face down attack. Generally this will be because a face down card has an effect on it copying another card, or it was given some pre-set values.
notes: this refers to face down cards that are attacks, not face down cards that used to be attacks and then were flipped.
bullets: A face down attack that deals damage can still go to momentum during the End Phase.
A face down attack has copied the card type (attack), so it counts as an attack in your card pool for effects and so on.
A face down attack has 0 printed speed, 0 printed damage, and its printed zone is mid. (See: Printed)
When a card is played face down as a copy of an attack, its initial current state has all of the copied values. (It does not start at zeroes/nulls and get a modification)

name: Face Down Cards
definition:
In the Stage or Card Pool

A face down card in the stage is a blank foundation. It has no printed properties.

Any cards that were added to the stage face down, or became face down, during the current turn must be kept distinct for the duration of the turn.

A face down card in the card pool is just a blank card. It adds to progressive difficulty but does nothing else on its own.

If a face down card goes to the stage or card pool (unless from the hand), it stays face down by default.
bullets: You can look at your own face down foundations, but not your rival's.
You can look at face down cards in your card pool.

On following turns, face down cards may generally be left indistinguishable from one another. (Unless needed to track assigned effects, to count the number of times the card has been readied, or similar.)
A card that is played face down into the card pool as another card type will be treated as that card type during the End Phase.

name: Fired Effect
definition:
Fired effects are one of the four effect classes. (An effect's "class" dictates how or when it is applied, among other things)

A fired effect is an effect that triggers off of some game event. They're like response abilities, but they don't have to be played.

Release's "Your check to block this attack gets +2." is a fired effect.

Texas Smash's "If this attack deals damage, build the next foundation you play face up." is a fired effect that makes another fired effect.)

Fired effects happen before response abilities can be played.

If more than one fired effect triggers at once, the active player handles theirs first, then the inactive player. Each player handles their effects in any order they want.

By default, fired effects only trigger and execute once. Some detached or assigned fired effects can fire multiple times. If so, the text will make it clear.

Fired effects can create other effects.

A card involved in a fired effect's trigger could change zones (due to another effect or cost) after the effect triggers, but before it executes. If this happens, the effect cannot move it again, or otherwise interact with it. It may still read properties from it. (see: Scope)

An attack in the card pool may turn face down after a fired effect triggered by its resolution triggers, but before it executes. If this happens, the effect cannot move it or otherwise interact with it. It may still read properties from it.
examples: Binding Kick's "If this attack deals damage, draw 1 card." is a fired effect.
"For the rest of this turn, whenever an attack deals damage, gain 1 health." would fire multiple times.
notes: This is specific to attacks flipping after they resolve, and does not apply to other situations where a card is flipped
bullets: You can't choose not to trigger a fired effect. If one is active, it will always fire if the trigger occurs. Fired effects may have options as part of their text.
Effects that change future checks (such as "your next check gets +2") are fired effects that trigger when you make the check.
Any modifier effects (if applicable) are handled before fired effects. (see: Modifier Effects)

name: Flash
definition:
Flash is a keyword ability.

Flash grants "Skip this attack's Enhance Step."

If an attack gains Flash during the Enhance Step, the Enhance Step ends. Move to the Block Step.

name: Flip
definition:
To flip a card is to turn it face down. (see: Face down cards)

You can't flip a card that's already flipped.

This also means that if, for example, it has been readied this Combat Phase, it still counts as such.)
notes: Flipping or unflipping a card does not make it a "new card". Its current state refreshes, but it is still the same card. Any assigned effects remain on it.

name: Form
definition:
Forms consist of two different actions.

Form abilities are played during the Combat Phase, by the active player when they have priority to play forms.

As the active player, you have priority to play a form (or pass) whenever:
bullets: Playing a card
Playing a Form ability on a card.
There is no ongoing attack sequence
There are no response windows open
There are no cards or abilities in the middle of being played or executing

name: Form Ability
definition:
Form abilities are played during the Combat Phase, by the active player when they have priority to play forms.

An form ability is playable if it's on a ready card in the stage, or an action card in the hand.

name: Foundation
definition:
Foundations are one of the six card types.

You play foundations as a form into the card pool during the Combat Phase, and they get built into the stage during the end phase.

Foundations can be used to pass checks.

(see: Making a Check)

If a card counts or refers to your foundations, it means foundations in your stage.

name: Freeze
definition:
To "freeze" a card is to create the assigned rule effect "That card does not ready during its owner's next Ready Step."

You cannot freeze a card that is currently frozen.

name: Frenzy
definition:
Frenzy is a keyword ability.

Frenzy grants "Blitz: This attack gets +1 speed and +1 damage for each attack that has been blocked this turn."

name: Fury
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Gaining Abilities
definition:
Any effect that causes a card to gain the abilities of a second card will grant only the abilities printed on that second card at the time that the effect executes.

Gained abilities are part of a card's current state. Therefore, if the card changes zones or the turn ends, the card will lose the gained abilities, as its current state refreshes.

name: Gaining Keywords
definition:
Some effects will cause a card to gain keywords. A card can gain a keyword it already has.

Shoto Todoroki II can cause an attack with 'Stun: 2' to gain a second instance of 'Stun: 2'. Both instances of "'Stun: 2' can be played as separate enhances.)
examples: Neito Monoma can cause an attack that has "Taunt" to gain a second instance of 'Taunt".

name: Gaining Character Traits
definition: Some effects will cause a card to gain character traits.

name: Game Event
definition:
A "game event" is something that can trigger a response ability or a fired effect.

In some cases, game events are combined, delayed or prevented:

The nature of a game event can't change after it happens. In other words, if something triggers, it can't "un-trigger" because something changed before it was played or executed.

For the purposes of game events and otherwise, players don't act upon their rival's cards. If an instruction says to interact with a rival card, you choose the card if needed, then your rival completes the act. (see: Instructions)
examples: The response on Recipro Burst triggers "After your rival commits 1 or more foundations during this attack's Enhance Step". This triggers if rival foundation(s) are committed during the Enhance Step for any reason. Even if it was due to your effect, and even if you chose the card they committed.
bullets: Interacting with multiple cards at once is one game event.
Any game events that would happen while a player pays a cost wait until the payment is done. Then they happen as one collective event.
Any game events that would happen during a pause condition wait until the pause is over. Then they happen as one collective event.
Game events can't happen before the game starts.

name: Game Zones
definition: There are eight game zones. Both players have one of each. See their respective entries for details.
bullets: Card Pool
Deck
Discard Pile
Hand
Momentum
Removed From Game
Stage
Transit Zone

name: [Hand]
definition: [Hand] is a play restriction/permission that applies to some abilities on cards. Abilities with [Hand] in their text are subject to the following rule:
bullets: Abilities with the [Hand] restriction/permission may only be played from the player's hand, and they do not inherently require a check to be played. Abilities with [Hand] may be played without making a check unless otherwise specified.

name: Hand
definition:
The Hand is one of the game zones.

The cards in your hand are private information. You can look at them at any time, but not your rival.

There is no limit to how many cards you can have in your hand.

If an effect makes you reveal or move a card that meets a condition from your hand, and you don't have any, reveal your hand to verify this.
bullets: The number of cards in your hand is public information.

name: Hand Size
definition:
On a character, the number over the "cards" icon is their hand size.

You draw up to your hand size during the Draw Step. (see: Draw Step)

Your hand size is not a maximum. You can have any number of cards in your hand at any time. It only dictates how many cards you draw during the Draw Step.

name: Health
definition:
On a character, the number over the "heart" icon is their health.

You start the game with health equal to your character's health.

You can lose health due to costs or effects, or by taking damage. (see: Damage)

If a player is at 0 health or less, they lose the game.

Unlike hand size, health is a maximum.
bullets: Losing health for any reason other than a successful attack does not count as taking damage.
If an effect would gain health beyond the maximum, it gains up to the maximum instead.
If an effect would gain health but you are already at the maximum, it fails to gain health at all.
You can never have health above your maximum. If your maximum health is reduced below your current health, your current health is also reduced to the new maximum.

name: Identity
definition:
A character's identity is the primary information of the card name, excluding parentheses.

Different characters with the same identity will have different versions. (see: Version)

Characters with the same identity and version as your starting character can be built and attached. (see: Character)

If an effect names a character identity, any version of that character counts.

Identities are unique to a given property.

name: If
definition: An "if" condition in an effect applies to the rest of the sentence. Any following sentences are not subject to the condition.
examples: Even if you fail to remove a momentum with Capture Evil-Doers, it still removes itself.

name: In Its Name
definition:
If a card checks for a word in another card's name, it is checking for exactly that word.

(For example, "Red Riot's the Coolest" cannot apply the bonus from its Deadlock Enhance to "Measured Violence".)
bullets: If the letters of that word are contained in another word, it does not count.

name: Infinity
definition:
Cards with infinity have no symbols. Such cards can be played in any deck.

Characters with Infinity must select a Primary Symbol at deck construction. They do not have access to any other symbols during deckbuilding.

name: Instruction
definition:
Instructions are the steps that are part of a cost, or an effect.

By default,

If an instruction says to interact with a rival card, you choose the card if needed, then your rival completes the act.

Some instructions can be overridden by text later in the effect. (see: Effects)
examples: You use the first enhance on Electric Jolt, which instructs you to "Commit 1 rival foundation." You choose the foundation, and then they commit it.
bullets: If an instruction asks you to interact with card(s), it means your own cards.
You make any choices in the instructions of your own costs or effects. This includes choosing which card(s) to interact with if applicable.

name: Keyword
definition:
A card's keywords, if any, are located at the edge of its text box.

A keyword can be a keyword trait, or a keyword ability. Traits will be in white, abilities will be colored.

(see: Keyword Trait, Keyword Ability)

name: Keyword Ability
definition:
Keyword abilities are colored terms at the edge of the text box.

Each keyword ability grants some ability to the card that it's on.

Some keywords have a colon and a number after it. This number is a Keyword Rating. (see: Keyword Rating)

This is the list of different keyword abilities in the Universus CCG. See:
bullets: Orange keyword abilities grant enhances.
Green keyword abilities grant responses.
Pink keyword abilities grant blitzes.
Blue keyword abilities grant continuous abilities.
Breaker
Combo
Deflect
Desperation
Echo
Elusive
EX
Flash
Frenzy
Powerful
Safe
Shift
Stun
Terrain
Throw
Unique

name: Keyword Rating
definition:
Some keywords have a colon and a number after it. This number is a Keyword Rating.

Keyword abilities that have ratings will reference them in their text.

If an effect modifies a card's Keyword Rating, it only modifies 1 instance of the rating. If the card has more than 1 instance of the keyword, choose 1 rating to modify.

name: Keyword Trait
definition:
Keyword Traits are white terms at the edge of the text box.

Keyword traits have no inherent function, but cards may reference them.

These traits appear in the Universus CCG:

A subset of Keyword Traits includes Character Traits. Character Traits can only exist on Character cards. (see: Character Traits)
bullets: Ally
Armor
Charge
Diplomacy
Doopler
Fury
Kick
Pizza
Punch
Ranged
Ship
Slam
Spell
Taunt
Tech
Titan
Vestige
Weapon
XP

name: Kick
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Level Up
definition: Level up is an instruction to advance a skill tree and unlock the next ability on that skill tree.

name: Live Card
definition:
Any card that is face up, revealed, and/or in the stage or card pool, is live.

Live cards have a current state. (See: Current State)

Static effects on live cards are active. (See: Static Effect)
bullets: This excludes characters that aren't in the Stage.

name: Making a Check
definition:
To make a check, discard the top card of your deck. The discarded card has been checked. Look at the large blue check value in the lower right corner of the card. This is the starting value of the check.

The final value of the check is the starting value, plus or minus any changes that are made to it.

If you were checking against a difficulty, and the final value meets or exceeds it, the check passes.

If the final value is less than the difficulty needed, decide if you want to commit cards to pass it. You can commit foundations and/or your character.
examples: You've used the Enhance on Summon Dark Shadow to give your next check +3. When you go to play your next card, you check a 5. This is the starting value, then it gets +3 to make a final value of 8.
If you check a 4 to play a block on a difficulty of 5, you may commit High Value Target to pass the check, even though it would result in "committing more than you need" to pass the check.
bullets: You must be able to pass the check to commit cards.
You can't commit more or less than what you need. ○ As an exception, if you only need to add 1 to your check, you may commit a card that counts as more than card to pass the check.
All cards are committed at the same time.
If you can't or choose not to commit cards, the check fails.
If an effect makes a check automatically pass, skip making a check altogether.

name: Mill
definition: Mill means to put the top card of your deck into your discard pile. If the instruction to Mill is followed by a value, you will put that many cards into your discard pile instead.

name: Modifier Effect
definition:
Modifier effects are one of the four effect classes. (An effect's "class" dictates how or when it is applied, among other things)

A modifier effect applies some change to the current state of one or more cards. In other words, it changes some number or card property. (see: Current State)

Modifier effects apply as soon as an applicable card is live. This happens before any fired effects or response abilities. (see: Live Cards)

If more than one modifier effect is to apply at the same time, the active player applies theirs first, then the inactive player. Each player applies their effects in any order they want.

Modifier effects can have requirements. They will fail to apply if those requirements are not met.

By default, modifier effects apply one time. If a detached or assigned modifier effect can apply to multiple cards, the text will make it clear.
examples: : If you use Note Taking's "Enhance Flip: Your Punch attack gets +2 speed." during an attack that isn't a Punch, it will fail.

Kamui Woods' enhance makes a detached modifier effect that applies to all attacks. If you play it during one of your attacks, that attack gets +1 damage right away. Your later attacks will get +1 damage as soon as they're played.
notes: If an attack is being played, it is live as soon as you announce it and it enters the transit zone. Changes to its speed, damage or zone are suspended until it's played successfully.
bullets: No modifier effect can be applied to the same card twice, until that card's current state gets refreshed or replaced.

name: Momentum
definition:
Momentum is one of the game zones.

Your momentum is a "bar" of cards next to your character. They can be arranged vertically or horizontally, or in any ordered fashion.

Momentum is a private zone, with some cards having the ability to add themselves or other cards to momentum face up. By default, cards are added to momentum face down.

When you add a card to momentum, you may add it in any position in the order. Cards added to momentum are added face down.

When an instruction causes a card to leave your momentum, by default, you can choose any card(s) you want. Some instructions will allow a rival to choose instead.

Other than adding or moving cards from it, the order of your momentum can't be changed.
bullets: You aren't allowed to mark or distinguish momentum aside from this ordering.

name: Mulligan
definition:
Before the game begins, each player gets a chance to take a mulligan, starting with the player going first.

a. The first player may mulligan their entire hand. If their new hand does not contain any foundations, they may reveal it and mulligan a second time. b. The second player may choose any number of cards in their hand to mulligan, only one time. c. After mulligans, any player who mulliganed must shuffle their deck.

(see: Starting the Game)
bullets: Starting with the first player, each player may mulligan. To mulligan cards, put the cards on the bottom of your deck and draw up to your hand size.
The game begins. The player who goes first skips the Start Phase of their first turn. Neither player may attack on their first turn of the game.
On their first turn only, the second player may draw 1 additional card during the Draw Step. This means the second player will draw above their starting hand size.

name: Naming Cards
definition: If you need to name a card, if you can uniquely identify it by other properties, that is also sufficient.

name: Negative Numbers
definition:
The Universus CCG allows numbers to be negative.

Attacks do not deal negative damage - if an attack would deal negative damage, it does not deal damage.

The only thing that can't be negative is X. (see: X)

If your rival tries to block it with a +3 low block modifier, their block will be against a difficulty of 1 (-2 + 3), plus progressive.

If you play the enhance on your -2 speed Recipro Burst, X is calculated to be -5 (-2 minus 3). But X can't be negative, it's zero instead.)
examples: Your rival gives your Recipro Burst -3 speed and then -2 speed, and you haven't increased it yet. Recipro Burst is now -2 (negative two) speed.
bullets: If X is defined in a way that would make it negative, it's 0 instead.
If you get to name a number for X, you can't name a negative number.

name: Next
definition: The "next attack" is either (a) the next attack a player tries to play, or (b) the next attack to enter an attack sequence, whichever happens first. Your "next card" is the next card a player tries to play, and so on.

name: Non-Character
definition: A non-character ability (or ability type, i.e. "non-character Enhance") is an ability that's not on a character card. A non-character effect is an effect that does not come from an ability on a character card.

name: Once per game
definition:
[Once per game] is a play restriction. (see: Play Restrictions)

[Once per game] means "Only one copy of this ability may be played per player, per game."
bullets: A copy of an ability is an ability with the same cost and effect, on a card with the same name.

name: Once per turn
definition:
[Once per turn] is a play restriction. (see: Play Restrictions)

[Once per turn] means "This ability may only be played once per turn."

name: Or
definition: A "<trait> or <trait>" card is a card that matches either description. If an effect impacts all "<trait> or <trait>" cards, it affects all cards that match either description. (you don't have to pick one)

name: Order of Operations
definition: As a summary, if multiple different types of things could be happening at once, handle them in this order:
bullets: Pause conditions
Current state creation / refresh
Effect expiration
Modifier effects
Fired effects
Opening a response window

name: Outwit
definition:
An Outwit ability is a certain type of response ability with [Outwit] in its cost. Outwit abilities can only be played while the card is face down in the player's stage. They have an inherent cost of "unflip and commit this card" in addition to any other costs.

Normally, face down cards in the stage are considered foundations with no abilities. A face down card in the stage with an Outwit ability printed on its face retains its Outwit ability, but all other abilities and effects treat that card as a foundation with no abilities.

name: Owner
definition:
The owner of a card is the player who started the game with that card in their deck.

The owner of an effect is:
bullets: Cards cannot change ownership during the game. If a card would move to a zone of a player that doesn't own it, it stays where it is instead.
The owner of the card that has the effect (if it's a static effect)
The person who played the ability (if it's a direct effect)
The owner of the effect that made the effect (otherwise).

name: Partially Blocked
definition:
You can block an attack with a card whose block zone is adjacent to (one zone away from) the attack zone. When this happens, the attack becomes partially blocked during the second part of the Block Step. (see: Block Step)

Partially blocked attacks deal half their damage (rounded up) during the Damage Step.

name: Pause Conditions
definition:
Pause conditions are situations that halt the game until they've been handled.

Any game events that would happen during a pause condition wait until the pause is over. Then they happen as one collective event.

If multiple pause conditions happen at the same time, handle them in the order listed below.

The pause conditions are:

card with Unique in the stage, that player sacrifices the unflipped copies.

If an effect would cause a pause condition to be unable to resolve, the pause condition tries to resolve and then ends. It does not pause the game indefinitely.
examples: The effect on Gravity Well is allowed to resolve, preventing foundations from being destroyed for the rest of the turn. One of the players then builds a second copy of a Unique foundation. Normally, the game would pause to make that player sacrifice 1 copy of said foundation. Because of the effect, the player does not sacrifice the foundation and the pause state ends. At the start of the next turn, the effect will wear off and the pause condition will resume, forcing that player to sacrifice 1 copy of the Unique foundation.

If an effect prevented a player from losing the game when they have 0 or less health, the game would pause and check for health at 0 or below. But because of the effect, it would be unable to resolve the pause condition. Play would then resume.
bullets: A player at 0 or less health loses the game.
A player with no cards in their deck cycles it. (see: Cycling) ○ If they cannot complete the game act of cycling, they lose the game
If a character is in a stage, or in transit en route to the stage, and it's not a copy of the starting character, move it to the discard pile. (see: Transit Zone)

If a player has 2 or more copies of the same card with Unique in their stage, they sacrifice 1 copy that was most recently added. ○ If 1 or more face down cards are unflipped, and the unflipped card(s) results in 2 or more copies of the same
