If there are 2 or more cards with Terrain in both stages combined, destroy the card(s) that have been in a stage while having the Terrain keyword for longer. ○ If 2 or more terrains are built or gain Terrain at the same time, the active player chooses which to destroy.
If there is an attack or action in a player's stage, discard it.

name: Penalty
definition:
A penalty is a -# decrease to some number.

If a number decreased via some means other than giving it -#, that is not a penalty.
examples: "This attack gets -2 speed" gives an attack a speed penalty. "Your rival's attack gets a speed penalty equal to half its speed (rounded up)" will give a 5 speed attack -3 speed.
If you use "You're So Obvious" to bring a 10 damage attack down to its printed damage (say, 6), that is not a damage penalty.

name: Phases & Steps
definition:
Phases, steps and turns cannot begin or end while effects are being executed, or a response window is open.

If an effect ends a phase or step, it ends as soon as the above is true.

Triggers can occur in between phases or steps, but not between turns.

name: Place On Top Of
definition:
If a card is placed on top of another card in the stage, the effect that caused this will specify a duration.

The top card temporarily replaces the bottom card. This includes superseding its printed properties, and getting a new current state.
bullets: This also means you don't have access to any abilities on the bottom card.
It's still one card for the purposes of any sort of counting.

name: Play a [Card Type]
definition:
If a rule or card text refers to "play(ed/ing) a [card type]", it means to play the card as that type, and not as a block.

Playing a card for any reason (block or not) counts as "play(ed/ing) a card", or "play(ed/ing) a [card type] card".

The first triggers "After you play this card". This triggers when you play the card for any reason - as an attack, or as a block.

The second triggers "After you play this attack". This triggers only if you play the card as an attack. )
examples: Specialized Sound Waves has two response abilities.

name: Play Restrictions
definition:
Play restrictions limit or change how or when you can play the ability they're associated with. They can appear in a few different forms.

A play restriction can appear after effect text. These are not part of the effect.

Some play restrictions appear in brackets after the type of an ability.

○ [Your/Rival's turn] Only playable during your / your rival's turn.

Other play restrictions appear in bold before the type designation.
notes: Passing does not count. If you pass your first chance to play an enhance, you can still play a First Enhance on your next chance.
bullets: "Only playable (condition)" - The given condition must be met to play the ability.
"Playable while committed." - You can play this ability even if the card it is on is committed. (It can be played if the card is ready too)
"This ability cannot be canceled." - Any instruction to cancel the ability fails.
"This enhance is playable any number of times this Enhance Step." - The ability ignores the normal one use per Enhance Step limit.

"Your rival may (instructions) to cancel this ability." - After you play the ability, your rival can choose to do as specified. If they do, cancel the ability. ○ If your rival takes the specified act, treat it as a cost, not part of your effect. ○ Your rival decides this after any fired effects, but before response abilities that trigger on the ability being played.
If the brackets contain a zone, you can only play the ability when the card is in that zone. ○ This also allows it to be played from that zone, even if it normally wouldn't be.

Otherwise, the brackets can have one or more restrictions. These can be: ○ [Once/Twice per turn] Only playable once/twice per turn. ○ [Your/Rival attack] Only playable during your / your rival's attack.

"First" - The ability has to be the first act of that type. ○ A "First Form" is only playable if you haven't done any other forms yet that Combat Phase. (see: Combat Phase) ○ A "First Enhance" is only playable if you haven't tried to play any other enhances yet that Enhance Step.
"Combo" - You can't play the ability unless you meet the requirements of the card's Combo keyword ability. (see: Combo)
"Deadlock" - You can't play the ability unless your rival has more than 10 (so, 11 or more) foundations.

A character name(s) means you can only play the ability if your character has that name. ○ Some character name restrictions are shortened to save space. They will be enough to indicate a specific character identity.
A symbol icon means you can only play the ability if that symbol is on your starting character.

A set icon means you can only play the ability if your character has that set icon. ○ Additionally, if a continuous ability is preceded by a set icon, it is only active if your starting character has that set icon. This restriction applies despite the fact that continuous abilities are not played.

name: Played Ability
definition:
A played ability is an ability that's activated by the player who owns the card it is on.

Played abilities consist of:

For each card type, the zone their played abilities are normally usable in is:

You can't activate a played ability on a committed card.

If a card changes zones, any count of how many times its abilities have been played is reset.
bullets: A type designation (Enhance, Form or Response) along with any restrictions (see: Play Restrictions)
A cost, if there is any
A colon
An effect (see: Direct Effect)
Additional restrictions, if any (see: Play Restrictions)
In some instances, a bulleted list of options (see Bullets in Abilities)
Characters - Stage
Foundations - Stage
Assets - Stage
Attacks - Card Pool
Actions - Hand

name: Playing an Ability
definition:
To play an ability, first you announce that you're going to play it.

Then you pay all costs, in order.

After costs are paid (and any triggers off of the cost are handled), the ability is played. Begin executing its effects.
notes: The playing of an ability is a common response trigger. If your rival has any responses like that available, make sure you give them a chance to use them.)wait
bullets: You can't declare an ability that you can't pay the cost for. Or one that is disallowed for some other reason.
Nothing triggers in the middle of paying costs - all game events that would occur wait. At the end of cost payment, they occur as a single event. (see: Game Event)

name: Playing a Card
definition:
To play a card, first you announce that you're going to play it.

Then place the card face up on the table. It waits in the transit zone while you go through the process of playing it. (see: Transit Zone) If playing a card from your card pool, it stays in the card pool instead.

Now, determine the difficulty you will be checking against.

If you're playing an action, pay the ability's costs now(if any), in order.

Make a check against the difficulty needed. (see: Making a Check)
bullets: If you are playing an attack, and the rival has one or more backups in their stage, declare whether you are attacking the player's character or the specific backup you would like to attack.
If you're playing an ability on an action card, you are playing the ability and the card in one sequence. You can't declare an ability on an action that you can't pay the cost for.
For a card you're playing as its card type: This equals its difficulty value, plus Progressive Difficulty.
For a card you're playing as a block: This equals your card's block modifier, plus the attack's speed, plus Progressive Difficulty.
If playing a card from the card pool, the card you are playing does not count towards Progressive Difficulty.
Nothing triggers in the middle of paying costs - all game events that would occur wait. At the end of cost payment, they occur as a single event. (see: Game Event) 38
If you fail the check, discard the card. Your attempt to play it failed.
If you pass the check, put the card in the rightmost spot of your card pool (unless it changed zones due to paying a cost). It is now played.

name: Powerful
definition:
Powerful is a keyword ability.

Powerful grants "Enhance Discard 1 or more momentum: This attack gets +X damage for each card used to pay the cost of this ability. X equals the rating of the Powerful keyword granting this ability."

The Powerful rating is the number next to the Powerful keyword. "Powerful: 2" has a Powerful rating of 2.

name: Preceding
definition:
A preceding card in the card pool is any card to the left of the referenced one.

If an effect looks for a "directly preceding", "immediately preceding", or "the preceding" card(s), it means the card(s) directly to the left.

name: Printed
definition:
A card's printed properties are the ones physically printed on it.

If an effect checks a printed property of a face down card:
examples: : A stun card has an enhance ability printed on it.
bullets: This includes applying any rules that interpret them.
The printed value is zero if it's a number
The printed property is considered mid if it's an attack or block zone
The printed property is null otherwise

name: Private
definition:
Private cards are cards that only you can look at.

Cards in your hand, and face down cards in your stage, card pool, and momentum are private.

If you get to look at a card, it's temporarily private.

name: Progressive Difficulty
definition:
When playing a card, Progressive Difficulty is a value equal to the number of other cards in your card pool.

(The card you are playing does not enter the card pool until you have finished playing it, but we refer to "other cards in your card pool" for clarity reasons.)

name: Properties
definition:
The properties of a card are:

A card's current state tracks its properties. (See: Current State)
bullets: Name
Version (characters)
Set Icon
Health or Stamina
Hand Size (characters)
Difficulty
Check Value
Block Zone
Block Modifier
Keywords
Abilities
Speed (attacks)
Attack Zone (attacks)
Damage (attacks)
Symbols

name: Public
definition:
Public cards are cards either player can look at.

Any face up card is public.

If an effect reveals a private or unknown card, it is public temporarily.
notes: All public cards are live. See: Live Card

name: Publicity
definition:
A card's "publicity" means whether or not it is public.

If a card's publicity changes, its current state refreshes. (See: Current State)

name: Punch
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Ready
definition:
A ready card is one in normal, portrait orientation.

You can't ready a ready card.

Only cards in the stage can be ready or committed.

name: Ready Step
definition:
The Ready Step is the first part of the Start Phase, before the Review Step.

During your Ready Step, ready all committed cards in your stage.

name: Re-check
definition:
If you are instructed to re-check after making a check, repeat the process of making a check. This new check replaces the previous check.

Any modifications to the original check do not carry over to the re-check.

You cannot re-check a second time after making a re-check.
notes: Re-checks will always occur before the player decides to commit additional cards to pass the check, in the instance that the check value is not sufficient to pass the check.

name: Remove
definition: "Remove" is often used as shorthand for "Remove from the game".

name: Removed From Game
definition:
Removed From the Game ("RFG" or "Removed" for short) is one of the game zones.

Your RFG zone should be off to the side of the other play areas.

Removed cards are public. Either player can look at them at any time.

Cards removed by an effect that specify they be removed face down are private.
notes: Despite the name, the Removed From Game zone is still a game zone, and can be part of the game. Effects might check for cards in it, or even take cards from it in rare cases. The RFG name mainly implies that removed cards don't normally get back into a player's hand or deck.

name: Resolve
definition:
An attack resolves after its attack sequence ends. (see: Attack Sequence)

Dropped attacks do not resolve. (see: Dropped Attacks)

name: Response Ability
definition:
Response abilities can be played after a specified event. Each response ability has its own trigger condition.

A response ability is playable:

Either player can open a "response window" any time a game event happens. (see: Game Event)

Response windows can interrupt effects, steps, or other response windows. If so, pause the interrupted process until the response window closes.

Once a response window opens, players alternate getting priority to play a response. The active player has priority first.

When a player has priority, they can play a valid response ability or pass.

Once both players pass in succession, the window closes.

A card involved in a response ability's trigger may change zones (due to another effect or cost) after the ability triggers, but before its effect executes. If this happens, the effect cannot move it again, or otherwise interact with it. It may still read properties from it. (see: Scope)

An attack in the card pool may turn face down after a response ability triggered by its resolution triggers, but before that ability's effect executes. If this happens, the effect cannot move it or otherwise interact with it. It may still read properties from it.
examples: Say a response ability reads "Response: After this card is destroyed, …". You can play that response even though the card is in the discard pile when it triggers.
notes: This is specific to attacks flipping after they resolve, and does not apply to other situations where a card is flipped
bullets: If it's on a ready card in the stage
If it's on an action card in the hand
If it's on the current attack from the time the card is successfully played until it resolves
If the response trigger references the card it is on
A response ability is valid if the game event that opened the window meets its trigger condition. (note: one game event can meet multiple conditions)
Each response ability can only be played once per response window.

name: Reveal
definition:
If a player reveals a card, it stays revealed until the effect that revealed it is done executing.

Revealed cards are public, and therefore live. (see: Live Cards)

Public cards may be revealed.
bullets: Also, both players must get a chance to read the card.

Revealing a face down action, attack, or character that does not share both the identity and version with your starting character in the stage does not cause the revealed card to be sent to the discard pile.

name: Review Step
definition:
The Review Step is the second part of the Start Phase. It comes after the Ready step and before the Draw Step.

During your Review Step, you may discard 1 card. The card discarded during the Review Step has been reviewed.
bullets: By default, only 1 card per turn (a card discarded during the review step) will count as being reviewed each turn. Game effects may modify this and allow other cards to count as being reviewed.

name: Rival
definition:
Your "rival" is your rival in the game.

Rival can be used as an adjective, such as "commit 1 rival foundation". This means 1 of your rival's foundations.

name: Ruin
definition: Ruin X is an instruction that means your rival must sacrifice X facedown foundations, or as many as possible if they have fewer face down foundations than the value of X.

name: Rule Effect
definition:
Rule effects are one of the four effect classes. (An effect's "class" dictates how or when it is applied, among other things)

A rule effect applies some added rule to the game while it is active.

"Your rival cannot play action cards during this attack." is a rule effect.)

Handle applicable rule effects before any other effects.

If there are multiple rule effects to handle at the same time, the active player applies theirs first, then the inactive player. Each player applies their effects in any order they want.

Effects that prohibit / prevent, take precedence in any conflict. It does not matter what order rule effects were applied in. (see: Conflict Resolution)
examples: : "If this attack is added to your momentum, add it face up." is a rule effect.

name: Sacrifice
definition: An effect that instructs a player to sacrifice a card means that player must choose and destroy a card in their stage that matches the instruction.

name: Safe
definition:
Safe is a keyword ability.

Safe grants the ability "Attacks cannot be played as a reversal to this attack."

Currently, "Reversal" only appears on reprinted cards from older formats.

name: Scope
definition:
An effect's scope defines its frame of reference.

If a card was moved or flipped or similar, but an effect needs to get a value from it, scope helps define what value to use.

When an effect checks the value of a card property:

Say you have an ability "Enhance Destroy 1 foundation: This attack gets +X damage. X equals the destroyed foundation's difficulty."

If you destroy a face down foundation for it (which has a difficulty of 0), you get +0 damage. Not whatever difficulty the card has now that it's face up in your discard pile.)

If an effect is created by another effect, it inherits the original effect's scope.

Otherwise,
notes: The above is a technical explanation, but in practice it's often intuitive.
bullets: Use the current value if the card hasn't changed zones or publicity during the effect's scope.
If the card has changed zones or publicity during the scope - use the last value the property had, in the first zone where it was live (during the scope).
The scope of a Form or Enhance ability's direct effect starts when the ability is announced.
The scope of a Fired Effect, or the direct effect of a Response ability, begins with the event that triggered it. 42

name: Seal
definition:
A sealed card has no abilities for the rest of the turn.

Specifically, to "seal" a card means to assign a rule effect to it that reads "this card has no abilities" until the end of this turn.

You cannot seal a card that is currently sealed.
notes: Keyword traits are not abilities! Sealing has no effect on them.
bullets: This also prevents the card from gaining abilities. (see: Conflict Resolution)

name: Search
definition:
To search a zone means to look through all the cards in that zone. This includes private or unknown cards.

If you are searching a zone for a card in order to put it in a destination zone, move the card you found to the transit zone. If searching for a card that meets some requirement(s), reveal it.

If you search a zone and fail to find a card that meets the instruction's requirement(s), you do not need to prove the absence.

If you search your deck for any reason, shuffle it afterwards.
notes: The card might be revealed in transit due to its origin or destination zone, regardless. See Transit Zone.

name: Set Icon
definition:
The set icon is on the bottom left of a card.

Some abilities can only be played by characters with a

certain set icon. (see: Play Restrictions)

Otherwise, set icons have no impact on gameplay.

name: Skill Tree
definition:
A Skill Tree is a list of abilities that can be granted to a character when they level up. A character using a Skill Tree will indicate which skill tree(s) they have access to. A character with a skill tree will also have at least one ability that causes them to level up. When a character levels up, they unlock the next ability listed on their skill tree. Unlocking means that character gains the unlocked ability for the rest of the game.

A Skill Tree is an extension of the character, though not specifically a character card itself. Skill Trees don't exist in any game zone.
bullets: This means that if the character is sealed, their unlocked abilities will return at the end of the current turn or whenever the predefined duration ends.
Unlocked abilities are not considered to be printed on the character card but are still character abilities.
Because Skill Trees don't exist in a game zone, they cannot be discarded, spent, removed, committed, frozen, sealed, destroyed, or any other effect that would apply a state to them.

name: Shift
definition: Shift is a keyword ability. Shift grants the continuous ability "As this card clears from your card pool during your End Phase, if you played it, you may build it transformed (opposite side up)."

name: Slam
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Speed
definition:
The number in the arrow icon of an attack is its speed.

When you try to block, the difficulty you check against is equal to: The speed of the attack + the block modifier of your block + progressive difficulty. (see: Progressive Difficulty)

Speed can be negative (see: Negative Numbers). It gets included in block difficulty with no modification.

(-2 + 3), plus progressive.)
examples: Say you have a -2 speed attack, and your rival wants to block with a +3 modifier. The difficulty they check against is 1

name: Spend
definition: To spend a card means to move it from your momentum to your discard pile.
bullets: If an effect is looking for a card to have been discarded from momentum, a card that has been spent counts, and vice versa.

name: Stage
definition:
The stage is one of the game zones.

Cards in the stage can be ready or committed.

(see: Ready, Committed)

Abilities on ready cards in the stage are playable.

(see: Played Ability)

To "build" a card means to add it to your stage. (see: Build)

You can arrange the cards in your stage in any way you'd like. So long as their presence and state (ready or committed) are clear to both players.

Any card that gets flipped, or built face down, must be kept distinct and identifiable for the rest of the turn.

If it's not specified, face down cards from zones other than the hand build face down. Anything else builds face up.
bullets: After that turn, they can be indistinguishable. Unless you need to track an assigned effect, or whether it's been readied, or similar.

name: Stamina
definition:
On a backup, the number over the green heart icon is their stamina.

When a backup enters the stage, it enters with maximum stamina.

A backup can lose stamina due to costs or effects, or by taking damage. (see: Damage)

When a backup has 0 or less stamina, destroy it.

Like health, stamina is a maximum.
bullets: Losing stamina for any reason other than a successful attack does not count as taking damage.
If a backup would gain stamina beyond the maximum, it gains up to the maximum instead.
If a backup would gain stamina but it is already at the maximum, it fails to gain stamina at all.
A backup can never have stamina above its maximum. If a backup's maximum stamina is reduced below its current stamina, its current stamina is also reduced to the new maximum.

name: Start Phase
definition:
The Start Phase is the first part of the turn, and comes before the Combat Phase.

The Start Phase has three steps. See their respective entries for details.

Ready Step

Review Step

Draw Step

name: Starting the Game
definition:
Before the game begins, go through these steps to set up.

1. Both players place their starting character in their stage. They put their deck in their play area. 2. Randomly decide who chooses the player to go first. The first player commits their character. 3. Players set their health to their character's maximum health. Keep track with pen and paper, tokens, a tracker or any other means. 4. Any fired effects that trigger "before the game begins" happen now. a. No other game events can happen before the game starts.

5. Players shuffle their decks until sufficiently randomized. They may shuffle or cut their rival's deck. 6. Players draw up to their character's starting hand size. 7. Starting with the first player, each player may mulligan. To mulligan cards, put the cards on the bottom of your deck and draw up to your hand size. a. The first player may mulligan their entire hand. If their new hand does not contain any

foundations, they may reveal it and mulligan a second time. b. The second player may choose any number of cards in their hand to mulligan, only one time. c. After mulligans, any player who mulliganed must shuffle their deck. 8. The game begins. The player who goes first skips the Start Phase of their first turn. Neither player may attack on their first turn of the game. 9. On their first turn only, the second player may draw 1 additional card during the Draw Step. This means the second player will draw above their starting hand size.
examples: Eraser Head adds a copy of ERASE! to his discard pile before the game begins. This is done before decks are shuffled.

name: Static Effect
definition:
A static effect is the effect of a continuous ability on a card. (see: Continuous Ability)

A static effect is active so long as the card it is on is live (and has the continuous ability).
bullets: As an exception, a static effect of a continuous ability on a character card is not active unless that card is a player's starting character.

name: Stun
definition:
Stun is a keyword ability.

Stun grants "Enhance: Your rival commits X foundations. X equals the rating of the Stun keyword granting this ability."

The Stun rating is the number next to the Stun keyword. "Stun: 2" has a Stun rating of 2.

A card committed by a stun effect can be referred to as a stunned card. Or, if a card ability references it being stunned, it is referencing being committed due to a stun effect.
notes: Your rival can't commit their character to stun! It's not a cost, so they can only commit foundations

name: Symbol
definition:
Each card has three symbols on the bottom left.

There are twelve different symbols, as well as infinity.

(see: Infinity)

Air All Chaos Death Earth Evil Fire Good Life Order Void Water

When deckbuilding, every card in your deck must share a chosen symbol, including your starting character. (see: Deck Construction)

This rule is adjusted by the new Attuned symbols. See: Attuned.

name: Tech
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Tenacious
definition: Tenacious means that the ability is playable while committed. Cards that have the text "Playable while committed" as part of an ability are considered to read as if they had the Tenacious term applied.

name: Terrain
definition:
Terrain is a keyword ability.

Terrain grants "After this card is added to your stage, destroy all other terrain cards."
notes: This is actually redundant. A pause condition destroys other Terrain cards anyway. (see: Pause Condition) But the ability has this text for clarity

name: This Attack
definition:
If an enhance, a response on the current attack, or a non-attack card, refers to "this attack", it's looking for the attack (if there is one) that is in progress. That is, an attack that's either in its attack sequence, being played, was just played, or just resolved (i.e. if something triggered on the attack resolving).

In a continuous or response ability on an attack, "This attack" only refers to the attack the ability is on.

"Your attack" or "Your rival's attack" (in any scenario) refer to the attack in a state of progress, provided it is either yours or your rival's, respectively.

name: Throw
definition:
Throw is a keyword ability.

Throw grants "If this attack is completely blocked, it deals half damage (rounded up) during the Damage Step."

name: Titan
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Title
definition: A title is a unique identifier used to represent a specific version of a character. A character's Title appears after, or underneath their name or identity on the character card.

name: Transform
definition:
To transform a card is to change it to its opposite side. Cards that transform have a default state of the front side being the face of the card.

If a transformed card leaves its current zone of play, changes facing, or changes publicity, it will return to the front side unless instructed otherwise by game text.

name: Transit Zone
definition:
The Transit Zone is one of the game zones.

The transit zone has no physical location. It's a temporary, abstract zone that holds cards as they go from one zone to another.

Most commonly, cards are in the transit zone while they're in the process of being played.

Cards in the transit zone can be public, private, or unknown. (see: Public, Private, Unknown)

If a card is being played, it gets a new current state as soon as it's in the transit zone.

If a card is in the transit zone for another reason, if it has a current state in its origin zone, it keeps it until it reaches its destination. Then the current state will refresh.

Going to and from the transit zone does not count as a zone change in and of itself. The zone change is the complete action of going from the origin zone, to transit, to the destination zone.

Cards that relocate within a zone do not go through the transit zone.
bullets: Less commonly, an effect may trigger after a card leaves a zone, or before a card arrives in a zone. At this point in time, it will be in the transit zone.
If the card was public in its original zone, or will be public in its destination, it's public in transit. (see: Public)
If the card is not public in transit, it's private if it was private in its origin zone, or will be in its destination. (see: Private)
Otherwise, unknown cards going to a zone where they will still be unknown, stay unknown in transit. (see: Unknown)
If the card turns face up when it enters the transit zone, its current state refreshes at that time as usual.
As mentioned above, entering transit counts as leaving the origin zone. A card will be in the transit zone "before entering" the destination zone.

name: Twice per turn
definition:
[Twice per turn] is a play restriction. (see: Play Restrictions)

[Twice per turn] means "This ability may only be played twice per turn."

name: Unblockable
definition: Attacks that are Unblockable cannot be blocked by any means. The defending player may not play a block during the block step and an effect cannot cause the block status to be set.

name: Unflip
definition:
To unflip a card is to turn a face down card face up.

These are newly gained abilities, even if the card was face up (and had those same abilities) earlier in the turn. You haven't played these new abilities yet. So, you can use things like [Once per turn] abilities. It doesn't matter what happened with the abilities the card used to have before it was flipped.)
notes: Face down cards in the stage have no abilities. If you unflip one, that card's current state will refresh, and it will now have abilities.
bullets: You can't unflip a face up card.

name: Unique
definition:
Unique is a keyword ability.

Unique grants "While this card is in your stage, if there are 2 or more copies of it in your stage, sacrifice the copy that was most recently added."

(see: Pause Condition) But the ability has this text for clarity.)
notes: This is actually redundant. A pause condition destroys duplicates of Unique cards anyway.

name: Unseal
definition:
To unseal a card means to remove the rule effect of Seal.

These are newly gained abilities, even if the card was face up (and had those same abilities) earlier in the turn. You haven't played these new abilities yet. So, you can use things like [Once per turn] abilities. It doesn't matter what

happened with the abilities the card used to have before it was sealed.)
notes: Sealed cards in the stage have no abilities. If you unseal one, that card's current state will refresh, and it will now have abilities.
bullets: You can't unseal a card that is not sealed.

name: Unknown
definition:
Unknown cards are cards neither player can look at.

Cards in your deck are unknown.

name: Version
definition:
On character cards, the version can be represented in one of two ways. The first is the roman numeral to the top right of the text box. The second is by the character having a Title. (see: Title)

You can only attach characters with the same identity and version as your starting character. (see: Character)

name: Weapon
definition: A keyword trait. (see: Keyword Traits)

name: Winning The Game
definition: You win the game if your rival is at 0 or less health, or is unable to cycle due to not having enough cards in their deck.

name: X
definition: When a card refers to X:
bullets: The effect may define what X is.
If not, X may have been part of a cost leading to the effect.
If X is not predefined, the player who owns the ability (for a cost) or effect can pick any valid number to be X.
X cannot be negative. If it would be negative for any reason, it is zero instead.

name: Zone Changes
definition: When a card changes game zones:
bullets: Assigned effects on that card end (see: assigned effects)
The card's current state refreshes
Any counting history on the card (i.e. whether the card has been readied, or has its once per turn ability been used, etc.) is reset.

name: Zones
definition: See:
bullets: Attack Zone
Block Zone
Game Zone
